description: FTP services are used to transfer files. In labs and CTFs, they may expose anonymous access, readable files, writable directories, backups, or credentials inside downloaded files.
why_prioritize: FTP can expose files directly, sometimes without authentication through anonymous login.
Files found through FTP often contain usernames, passwords, configuration details, source code, or clues for other services.
Writable FTP directories can reveal upload behavior that is useful to understand during enumeration.

enumeration_steps.title: Check the FTP banner and version
enumeration_steps.reason: The banner can reveal the FTP server software and version, which helps you understand what you are dealing with.

enumeration_steps.title: Check anonymous login
enumeration_steps.reason: Anonymous FTP access is common in labs and can expose files without valid credentials.

enumeration_steps.title: List directories and download interesting files
enumeration_steps.reason: The main value of FTP enumeration is often reviewing exposed files carefully.

enumeration_steps.title: Check write access safely
enumeration_steps.reason: Writable FTP directories can change the risk of the service, but you should test carefully in authorized labs only.
look_for: Whether uploaded files are accessible somewhere else

next_steps: List all accessible directories and files.
Download interesting files for offline review.
Check for credentials, usernames, hostnames, and internal paths.
Use any discovered information to guide enumeration of other services.
Search the files for passwords, tokens, database names, and usernames.
Check whether the same credentials work on SSH, web logins, SMB, or databases.
Look for application paths that map to web directories.
Take note of software names and versions mentioned in the files.
Confirm whether uploads are allowed using a harmless test file.
Check whether uploaded files can be downloaded back from FTP.
Look for a related web service that may expose the same directory.
Remove test files after checking to keep the lab clean.
Record the username, password, and where they were found.
Try the credentials carefully against related services in the lab.
Check whether the username appears in web pages, SMB shares, SSH, or database services.
Avoid assuming one credential works everywhere; verify service by service.
Search for known issues affecting that exact product and version.
Confirm the version with more than one source when possible.
Check whether the version is a lab hint or just outdated banner text.
Prioritize manual enumeration before assuming exploitability.

beginner_notes: Always try anonymous login in labs when FTP is open.
Do not only look at filenames. Download and read interesting files carefully.
Use binary mode when downloading non-text files through the FTP client.
If FTP exposes credentials, test them carefully and take notes of where they came from.
